title: Understand: The Divine Right of Kings and Social Contract Theory
description: Check your understanding of the Divine Right of Kings and Social Contract Theory in this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: Principles of the American Revolution: The Ideas and Events That Led to Change
The Divine Right of Kings and Social Contract Theory
Understand: The Divine Right of Kings and Social Contract Theory

assessment_items.question_content:
**The idea that the people give away some of their rights in exchange for protection by the government is known as what?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Social contract theory
Quid pro quo
The Great Compromise
The Corrupt Bargain

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the name for the idea that monarchs get their authority to rule directly from God?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Social contract theory
Democratic republic principle
Divine right of kings
Constitutional monarchy doctrine

assessment_items.question_content:
**According to social contract theory, what is the basis of governmental authority?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Divine right
Military force
The people
Property ownership

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did social contract theory influence Enlightenment thought?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: By suggesting that monarchs have absolute authority
By promoting the ideas of popular sovereignty and the consent of the governed
By emphasizing the importance of hereditary rule
By encouraging certain religious beliefs and practices

assessment_items.question_content:
**What argument does John Locke make in support of social contract theory?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: That kings are chosen by and get their authority from God, so they cannot be questioned
That direct democracy, in which all laws are passed by popular vote, best protects individual liberties
That obedience to authority is the primary duty of all citizens, regardless of the government’s actions
That governments exist to protect natural rights, and if they fail to do so, people have the right to rebel

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the main idea behind the New Hampshire Bill of Rights of 1784?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: To declare independence from Great Britain
To establish a national religion that would influence the law
To expand the powers of the monarchy
To protect individual liberties and limit government power

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why was the New Hampshire Bill of Rights important?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It provided an example of an early state constitution that laid out a powerful vision of natural rights and limited government.
It led to the abolition of state governments, creating a stronger national government.
It reinforced the divine rights of kings and asserted that the colonies would be better off under British rule.
It was the first document that called for dividing power among the legislative, executive, and judicial branches of government.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What argument did the New Hampshire Bill of Rights make about individual freedoms?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: That individual freedoms are less important than a strong national government
That certain natural rights cannot be taken away by the government
That they are privileges granted to certain classes of society
That they are granted by the government and can be taken away at any time

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which statement best defines “state of nature”?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The agreement between a government and its citizens
The condition of living without government
The purest and simplest form of government
The rights that every human is born with

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which statement best defines the relationship between revolution and the social contract?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A revolution cannot happen if the social contract is in place.
The social contract is possible only after a revolution.
A revolution may occur when the government breaks the social contract.
The social contract defines the terms of a successful revolution.

assessment_items.question_content:
**According to social contract theory, the government has the obligation to pursue the common good. In turn, the people have the obligation to \_\_\_\_\_.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: vote to keep that government in power
challenge all laws and governmental decisions
protect, defend, and uphold American democracy
respect, honor, and live under the law

assessment_items.question_content:
**Under social contract theory, in what circumstances can people refuse to obey the authority of the government?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: When a government changes leaders
When a law is ineffective at promoting change
When a government becomes tyrannical
When a law contradicts their religious beliefs
